exemplar.title: To what extent has the boycott influenced Starbucks' social and financial performance?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has the boycott influenced Starbucks' social and financial performance?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Clearly identifies âÃthiqueâ as the key concept and links it to stakeholder trust and responsible sourcing
Returns to ethical considerations in the STEEPLE analysis and dedicated ethics section
Connects ethics to brand reputation and consumer perceptions throughout much of the investigation
weaknesses: Discussion of ethics disappears in the financialâperformance subsection
Conclusion only references ethics in a general way rather than analytically
Integration is patchy rather than continuous throughout the report
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates strong understanding of the ethics concept with clear analytical links in multiple sections, fulfilling most Band 4 descriptors. However, the concept is not sustained in every subsection and the conclusion treats it superficially, preventing Band 5. A score of 4/5 reflects this partial but strong integration.

strengths: Three supporting documents provided, meeting IBâs 3â5 requirement
All documents are directly relevant to social and financial impact of the boycott
Sources come from reputable news outlets and supply quantitative data points
weaknesses: All sources are journalistic with no triangulation from industry reports or academic studies
Depth of evidence is acceptable but not extensive
Citation formatting across appendix entries is inconsistent
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With exactly three relevant, sufficiently detailed documents, the work aligns with a Band 3 performance. To reach Band 4, the student would need greater variety and depth (e.g., industry reports, audited figures) and more rigorous citation consistency. Thus, 3/4 is appropriate.

strengths: Selects a mainstream toolkit (STEEPLE, McClellandâs Needs Theory, Freemanâs stakeholder model)
Correctly applies each tool to sections of the research question
Demonstrates adequate understanding of theory functions
weaknesses: Selection is solid but lacks breadth (no financialâratio analysis, no breakâeven modelling)
Application sometimes asserts rather than demonstrates clear relevance (e.g., mixed methods section lacking detail)
Ethics table feels disconnected from the main STEEPLE analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The tools chosen are adequate and applied with reasonable relevance, matching Band 3 descriptors. To improve to Band 4, the student should broaden tool selection and deepen application (more justification for choices, integration of quantitative models). Hence, 3/4.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Narrative summaries effectively link data tables back to the research context
Preâ and postâboycott graphs illustrate key shifts in customer behaviour
Survey question prompt is clear and supports relevance to the research question
Revenue forecast comparison adds nuance to the financial analysis
weaknesses: Several charts lack axis labels and data-source citations
Some data assertions (e.g., $11 billion market loss) are uncited
Analysis occasionally asserts causality without statistical testing or variance discussion
Ethical lens integration into analysis remains intermittent
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis goes beyond basic description, offering mostly effective, wellâintegrated interpretation of data and linking findings to the research question, consistent with Band 4. Minor lapses in citation and methodological rigor prevent Band 5, so a score of 4/5 is merited.

strengths: Conclusion restates key social and financial effects in line with earlier evidence
Partial answer to the research question is presented
weaknesses: Wording remains tentative and overly general
No quantified criteria or thresholds to address âÃ  quelle mesureâ explicitly
Does not synthesise findings from all analytical tools
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is broadly consistent with the evidence but only partially answers the research question, matching Band 2 descriptors. It lacks specificity and comprehensive synthesis required for a Band 3. Therefore, 2/3 is appropriate.

strengths: Logical structure with clear section headings and comprehensive table of contents
IntroductionâMethodologyâAnalysisâConclusion flow is coherent
Annexes and appendices are signâposted effectively
weaknesses: Subheading formatting could better indicate hierarchical levels
Transitions between sections (e.g., into STEEPLE analysis) are abrupt
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reportâs organization is consistently appropriate and guides the reader effectively, fulfilling Band 2 criteria for structure. Minor formatting improvements could be made, but overall it merits the full 2/2.

strengths: All required IB presentation elements are present (title page, word count, research question, bibliography, appendices)
Word count and bibliography entries adhere to IB guidelines
weaknesses: Citation formatting is inconsistent (footnotes, superscripts)
Reference style varies between entries
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission includes every required component correctly, satisfying Band 2. Though citation style inconsistencies remain, these do not detract from overall adherence to presentation requirements. Thus, 2/2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The report follows a logical structure (IntroductionâMethodologyâAnalysisâConclusion), uses headings and a contents page, and annexes are sign-posted.  Despite language errors, the organization remains coherent, satisfying âappropriate structureâ (Band 2).

All three documents are directly relevant to the boycottâs social and financial impact and provide data points (sales â7 %, layoffs, social-media views).  Depth is acceptable but not extensive; the pieces are all journalistic and there is no triangulation with industry reports or audited figures, so the evidence base lacks range.  This fits Band 3 â ârelevant and sufficiently in-depthâ.
All IB-required elementsâtitle page, word count, research question, table of contents, bibliography, appendicesâare present.  Therefore Band 2 is warranted.
Exactly three supporting documents are placed in an appendix, satisfying the 3-5 range.  Therefore Band 3 is appropriate for quantity.

Application is generally correct (e.g., STEEPLE table links boycott to social media exposure; McClelland used to explain staff turnover), but depth is limited and some links to the research question are asserted rather than shown with data.  This corresponds to ârelevance not always clear but adequately appliedâ (Band 3).

Data include percentage sales declines, share-price movement, and primary survey results from 39 teenagers.  While credible, the selection is modest and some figures lack source citations in-text.  Use of the data is competent (graphs produced, figures referenced) but not rigorous.  Hence âadequate selection and useâ (Band 3).

The analysis links survey results to lost customer loyalty and uses STEEPLE findings to explain financial effects, showing some linkage to the research question.  However, argument depth is uneven, causality is asserted rather than tested, and integration of the ethical lens is intermittent.  This is best described as âsome analysisâ (Band 3).

The research question is partially answered (âle boycott a influencÃ©â¦â) but without depth or clear criteria for measuring âmesureâ.  The tentative wording and lack of definitive stance indicate only a partial response, matching Band 2.

The student selects STEEPLE, McClellandâs Needs Theory and Freemanâs stakeholder model.  This is an adequate, mainstream toolkit, but there is no financial-ratio analysis or break-even modelling; hence selection is solid rather than wide-ranging.  Band 3 (âadequate selectionâ) is appropriate.

The conclusion states that the boycott influenced Starbucks âdans une certaine mesureâ and briefly reiterates social and financial effects, so it is broadly consistent with earlier evidence.  Nonetheless, it remains general, offers no quantified impact, and does not synthesise findings from all tools.  This aligns with Band 2 â âsome conclusions consistent with evidenceâ.

The investigation focuses consistently on Starbucks, citing its market share, share-price movements, store closures and regional sales declines.  Nonetheless, analysis sometimes slips into generic statements about âany companyâ and lacks sustained cross-linking of each analytical point back to Starbucksâ strategy or performance metrics.  This meets âclear analysis â¦ presentedâ (Band 3) but not the deeper âpartial integration throughoutâ required for Band 4.

The student clearly identifies Ethics as the key concept, explains its relevance to stakeholder trust and responsible sourcing, and returns to ethical considerations in the STEEPLE table and the separate âConsidÃ©ration Ãthiquesâ section.  However, discussion of ethics disappears in the financial-performance subsection and the conclusion only links back in a general way, so integration is patchy rather than âthroughoutâ.  This aligns with the descriptor for Band 4 â strong understanding with partial integration.

annotations.comment: The clear section header helps guide the reader into the analysis section.
The research question is clearly presented but would benefit from operational definitions of 'performance sociale et financiÃ¨re' to focus analysis.
Including the word count enhances presentation adherence to IB requirements.
The student clearly identifies 'Ãthique' as the key concept; to strengthen integration, provide a precise definition and link to ethical theory early.
Consider adding indentation or formatting for subheadings to distinguish hierarchical structure more clearly.
The introduction provides relevant background on Starbucks but could integrate a brief mention of the boycott context earlier for coherence.
Citation formatting for footnotes is inconsistent; align superscript placement and reference style to improve academic presentation.
The table of contents is comprehensive and reflects the logical organization of the report, aiding navigation.
The methodology header is well placed but consider specifying research instruments and timeframes explicitly under this heading.
The description of STEEPLE and McClellandâs theory is appropriate but could be improved by briefly justifying why these tools suit the research question.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The narrative summary following the table consolidates findings well, linking back to the research context.
The isolated ethics row reiterates ethical tools but feels disconnected; integrate this within the broader STEEPLE instead of separate table.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The methodology section briefly describes mixed methods but lacks detail on survey design, sample selection, and data validity for primary research.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The sub-section title clearly indicates focus area; maintaining this clarity helps structure the analysis.
The social impact description includes relevant figures, but lacks source attribution for the $11 billion market value loss assertion.
Strength: multiple reputable news sources are used as supporting documents for social impact analysis.
The transition into the STEEPLE analysis is abrupt; consider a brief contextual sentence linking social impact analysis to the STEEPLE framework.
The STEEPLE table effectively organizes external factors, but including quantitative data within the table would strengthen analysis depth.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Participant demographics and survey methodology are insufficiently detailed; clarify sample age, recruitment, and questionnaire validity.
The stray mathematical expression â6\\overline{6}â appears out of context and should be reviewed or removed.
The concluding sentence of the section succinctly links social media effect to financial outcomes.
The pre- and post-boycott visit frequency graphs illustrate key shifts but the scale differences between charts are unclear.
The financial performance section is clearly labeled, guiding the reader to focus on quantitative analysis.
The chart visually represents timeline impact but lacks axis labels and data source, limiting interpretability.
The survey question prompt is clearly stated, supporting data relevance to the research question.
The analysis cites percentage declines but lacks discussion on statistical variance and wider market benchmarks.
The revenue forecast comparison adds nuance; consider citing actual figures from the footnote rather than estimates.
Use consistent citation style; vary source types to enhance evidence depth and triangulation.
The ethical considerations section integrates stakeholder theory well; ensure each point ties back directly to the research question.
The discussion of McClelland and stakeholder models shows tool application, but analysis would benefit from specific examples of employee reactions.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The conclusion restates findings but is overly general; include specific quantified insights to strengthen consistency with evidence.
The conclusion answers the research question partially but needs to articulate 'Ã  quelle mesure' with clearer criteria or thresholds.
The extension suggestion in this postscript is good for depth; consider framing it as a distinct 'Limitations and Further Research' section.
The reflection on strategic ethics is insightful but drifts into general advice; align this with the case context for coherence.
All required bibliography entries are present, satisfying the IB presentation requirements.
Supporting documents are listed in the appendix, meeting the required quantity but verify consistency of formatting across entries.
